core.models.performance_counters module

PERFORMANCE COUNTERS DATA MODEL

This module defines the PerformanceCounters data class used throughout Module 1 to store hardware performance counter readings.

The class provides: - Type-safe fields for all counter types - Derived metrics (IPC, cache miss rate) - Serialization methods

Author: Deepak Panigrahy

class core.models.performance_counters.PerformanceCounters(instructions_retired: int = 0, cpu_cycles: int = 0, cache_references: int = 0, cache_misses: int = 0, major_page_faults: int = 0, minor_page_faults: int = 0, context_switches_voluntary: int = 0, context_switches_involuntary: int = 0, thread_migrations: int = 0, branches: int = 0, branch_misses: int = 0, cpu_clock_ms: float = 0.0, task_clock_ms: float = 0.0, duration_ms: float = 0.0)[source]

Bases: object

Hardware performance counter readings from perf_events.

These counters provide insight into what the CPU was actually doing during the measurement interval. They help distinguish between: - Actual computation (instructions retired) - Memory bottlenecks (cache misses) - OS scheduler overhead (context switches)

Req 1.5: Instruction density (IPC) Req 1.6: Cache behavior Req 1.10: Page faults Req 1.43: Thread migrations

branch_misses: int = 0

Mispredicted branches.

branches: int = 0

Branch instructions executed.

cache_miss_rate() → float[source]

Calculate cache miss rate.

Higher miss rates indicate memory bandwidth pressure, which can increase energy consumption significantly.

Req 1.6: Cache behavior

Returns:

Miss rate between 0.0 and 1.0

Return type:

float

cache_misses: int = 0

Number of cache misses (LLC misses).

cache_references: int = 0

Number of cache references (LLC accesses).

context_switches_involuntary: int = 0

Involuntary context switches (scheduler preempted).

context_switches_voluntary: int = 0

Voluntary context switches (process yielded CPU).

cpu_clock_ms: float = 0.0

CPU time consumed in milliseconds.

cpu_cycles: int = 0

Number of CPU cycles elapsed.

duration_ms: float = 0.0

Duration over which counters were measured.

instructions_per_cycle() → float[source]

Calculate Instructions Per Cycle (IPC).

IPC tells us how efficiently the CPU was running: - High IPC (>1) means CPU was doing useful work - Low IPC (<0.5) suggests stalls (waiting for memory)

Req 1.5: Instruction density

Returns:

Instructions per cycle, or 0.0 if no cycles

Return type:

float

instructions_retired: int = 0

Number of instructions retired (actual work done).

major_page_faults: int = 0

Major page faults (needed disk I/O).

minor_page_faults: int = 0

Minor page faults (resolved in memory).

task_clock_ms: float = 0.0

Task elapsed time in milliseconds.

thread_migrations: int = 0

Thread migrations between CPU cores.

to_dict() → Dict[str, Any][source]

Convert to dictionary for serialization.

Returns:

Dict with all fields and derived metrics

to_json() → str[source]

Convert to JSON string.

Returns:

JSON string with pretty formatting

total_context_switches() → int[source]

Total context switches (voluntary + involuntary).

Req 1.12: Kernel context switches

Returns:

Total number of context switches

Return type:

int
